20: nai erchomai tachy (nai erchomai tachy): 'Surely I am coming soon.' The final direct speech of Christ in the canon is a promise of return. Tachy (soon/quickly): the eschatological urgency that has characterized the entire Revelation. The community's response — Amen, erchou Kyrie Iesou (Come, Lord Jesus) — is the Greek form of the Aramaic Maranatha (1 Cor 16:22; Didache 10:6). The canon ends with a promise and a prayer: Christ's 'I am coming' and the community's 'Come.' The entire Christian life is lived in this tension of promise-and-prayer, the already of Christ's victory and the not yet of his return.
1: kai edeixen moi potamon hydatos zōēs lampron hōs krystallon ekporeuomenon ek tou thronou tou theou kai tou Arniou: the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, proceeding from the throne of God and of the Lamb. The singular throne shared by God and the Lamb (also v. 3) carries Christological weight: Revelation insists on co-enthronement, not adjacent thrones. The river's source is the co-divine throne, echoing Ezek 47:1-12 (the temple-river flowing east bringing life) and Zech 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem). In Ezekiel's vision the river flows from the temple; here the throne replaces the temple (21:22) as the source of living water.
2:
en mesō tēs plateias autēs kai tou potamou enteuthen kai ekeithen xylon zōēs poioun karpous dōdeka kata mēna: the tree of life (xylon zōēs) standing on both sides of the river, bearing twelve fruits, one each month — monthly fruit, not seasonal. The perpetual and varied fruitfulness signals inexhaustible provision. Xylon zōēs (tree of life) is the Genesis tree (Gen 2:9; 3:22-24) from which humanity was barred after the Fall; the new creation restores unlimited access. The twelve fruits monthly is a temporal abundance that no natural growing season could sustain.

kai ta phyla tou xylou eis therapeia tōn ethnōn: "and the leaves of the tree for the healing of the nations." Therapeia (healing, therapy) applied to the nations — the tree's leaves heal at a national, not merely individual, scale. This picks up Ezek 47:12 (fruit for food and leaves for medicine/healing). The healing of the nations implies some form of ongoing restoration in the new creation, though the precise nature of this healing in an already-perfect world resists precise specification.
3:
kai pan katathema ouk estai eti: "and no curse will be there any more." Katathema (curse, accursed thing) — the curse of Gen 3:17-19 (cursed is the ground because of you) is the source of all human suffering, toil, death, and the frustration of creation (Rom 8:20-22). Its abolition is the reversal of the Fall's cosmic consequences. Paul's statement that "Christ became a curse for us" (Gal 3:13) is the atonement event that makes this eschatological abolition possible.

kai ho thronos tou theou kai tou Arniou en autē estai: the throne of God and the Lamb — singular throne, dual subjects. Grammatically striking: two persons, one throne. The Lamb's co-enthronement with the Father in the new Jerusalem is the final statement of Revelation's high Christology. Hoi douloi autou latreusousin autō: his servants will latreuō him — the verb specifically used for religious service and worship (Heb 12:28; Luke 2:37). The new creation is characterized by unmediated, ceaseless worship of the co-enthroned God and Lamb.
4:
kai opsontai to prosōpon autou: "they will see his face." The beatific vision — direct, unmediated, face-to-face knowledge of God. In the OT, seeing God's face was dangerous or impossible (Exod 33:20: "you cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live"; Gen 32:30: "I have seen God face to face and yet my life has been spared"). The new creation removes this barrier: the redeemed see the face of God directly because the old order of divine hiddenness has given way to full presence. This is the ultimate fulfillment of the priestly blessing (Num 6:26: "the LORD make his face shine on you").

kai to onoma autou epi tōn metōpōn autōn: his name on their foreheads — the opposite of the mark of the beast on the foreheads of his worshippers (13:16-17). The sealed 144,000 already bore God's seal on their foreheads (7:3; 14:1). In the new Jerusalem this identity-marking is permanent and visible: they belong to God and the Lamb, identified by name as his own. The name on the forehead is ownership, identity, and protection all at once.
5:
kai nyx ouk estai eti kai ou chreian echousin phōtos lychnou kai phōtos hēliou hoti Kyrios ho Theos phōtisei ep autous: no night, no lamp, no sun — the Lord God will shine on (phōtisei, illuminate) them directly. Phōtizō (to illuminate) is used of God's eschatological light (Isa 60:19 LXX; Rev 21:23). The abolition of created light sources is not loss but supersession: what the sun and lamp did partially and temporarily, God does fully and permanently.

kai basileusousin eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn: "they will reign for ever and ever." The saints' reign with Christ (20:6) is here made permanent and universal. Dan 7:18, 22, 27 promised that the holy ones of the Most High would receive the kingdom forever; Rev 22:5 declares this fulfilled. The redeemed do not merely dwell in the new creation but reign in it — the restoration of the royal image-bearing vocation of Gen 1:26-28, now fully realized.
6: hoi logoi pistoi kai alēthinoi: the authentication formula (also 19:9; 21:5). The repetition at the book's conclusion brackets the entire vision with divine guarantee: what was pledged at the outset of the epilogue is confirmed at its close. Kyrios ho theos tōn pneumatōn tōn prophētōn (the Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets): a distinctive title found only here in Revelation, identifying God specifically as the inspirer of prophecy. The one who sent his angel to show the prophets what must soon take place is the same God who has been speaking through the prophets all along — continuity between OT prophecy and Revelation's vision asserted in the divine title itself.
7:
idou erchomai tachy: "Behold I am coming quickly/soon." The first of three erchomai tachy sayings in the chapter (vv. 7, 12, 20) — a rhetorical triad building to the final exchange with the community in v. 20. Tachy (quickly, soon) has been the eschatological urgency marker throughout Revelation (1:1; 2:16; 3:11; 22:7, 12, 20). The imminent return is not a mistaken prediction but an eschatological stance: the church lives under the horizon of the returning Lord.

makarios ho tērōn tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: the seventh and final beatitude in Revelation (see 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:14). Tērōn (keeping, guarding, observing) — present participle: ongoing keeping, not a single act. The beatitude at the book's close mirrors 1:3 (blessed is the one who reads and hears and keeps these words), forming a frame around the entire Apocalypse.
8:
kagō Iōannēs ho akouōn kai blepōn tauta: the Seer's self-identification — "I, John, the one hearing and seeing these things." The emphasis on hearing and seeing is Revelation's epistemological claim: this is received revelation (heard and seen), not theological speculation. The same identification occurs at 1:9 where John situates himself in the persecuted community. The repetition here at the book's end confirms the same human witness for both the beginning and the conclusion of the vision.

kai hote ēkousa kai eidon, epesa proskunēsai emprosthen tōn podōn tou angelou tou deiknuontos moi tauta: John falls to worship before the revealing angel — the same error as 19:10 (where the angel gives the same rebuke). The repetition is not careless; it illustrates the persistent human tendency to misdirect worship toward the mediator of revelation rather than the source. The correct response to overwhelming revelatory experience is not prostration before the messenger.
9: kai legei moi Hora mē syndoulos sou gar eimi kai tōn adelphōn sou tōn prophētōn kai tōn tērountōn tous logous tou bibliou toutou tō theō proskunēson: "See that you do not do this! I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers the prophets and with those who keep the words of this scroll. Worship God!" The angel's self-description as syndoulos (fellow-servant, co-slave) places him in the same category as John, the prophets, and all who keep the book's words. The hierarchy of worship is absolute: tō theō proskunēson (worship God [alone]) — the angel enforces monotheistic worship by refusing it for himself. Proskyneō (worship, bow before) is the term Revelation reserves for God and the Lamb (4:10; 5:14; 7:11; 11:16; 19:4, 10; 22:8-9).
10: Mē sphragisēs tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: "Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll." This is the direct inversion of Dan 12:4 (sphragison to biblion, "seal the book, Daniel, until the time of the end"). Daniel was told to seal because the fulfillment was distant; John is told not to seal because ho kairos engys (the time is near). The unsealing of John's prophetic scroll signals that the Danielic "time of the end" has arrived in and through Christ — the seals of history are being opened (ch. 6) rather than imposed. The eschatological "near" is the church's permanent posture of readiness, not a chronological prediction about a specific date.
11: ho adikōn adikēsatō eti, kai ho rhyparos rhyparasthētō eti, kai ho dikaios dikaiosynēn poiēsatō eti, kai ho hagios hagiasthētō eti: the fourfold declaration of fixity — let the unjust continue unjust, the defiled continue defiled, the righteous continue righteous, the holy continue holy. This is not permission for sin but an announcement of eschatological ultimacy: at the threshold of judgment, moral states are confirmed. The call to repentance is complete; the time for reversals is over. The hardened will not be transformed by more time; the holy will not be endangered by the end's arrival. The final state of each is the continuation of their present direction, accelerated to completion.
12: idou erchomai tachy kai ho misthos mou met emou apodounai hekastō hōs to ergon estin autou: the second erchomai tachy (v. 7, 12, 20). Here paired with the judgment: ho misthos mou met emou (my reward/wages are with me) — the eschatological recompense brought by the returning Lord. Apodounai hekastō hōs to ergon autou (to render to each according to their work) cites the standard OT judgment formula (Ps 62:12; Prov 24:12; Isa 40:10) applied to Christ: the one who comes is also the one who judges. This links the parousia and the judgment as one event, not two sequential stages.
13: ego to Alpha kai to Omega, ho prōtos kai ho eschatos, hē archē kai to telos: the threefold divine identity formula applied to Christ — the fullest divine self-identification in the NT. Each pair captures a different aspect: Alpha/Omega (the entire alphabet of reality, from first to last letter), First/Last (temporal priority and finality — echoing YHWH's self-identification in Isa 44:6 and 48:12), Beginning/End (archē kai telos — ontological primacy and consummation). In 1:8 the Alpha-Omega title is applied to "the Lord God Almighty" (the Father); here all three pairs are applied to Christ. Revelation's Christological high point: the crucified and risen Jesus is identified with YHWH's own divine self-designation.
14:
makarioi hoi plynnontes tas stolas autōn: "Blessed are those who wash their robes." The sixth beatitude in Revelation. An important textual variant: some MSS (including Codex A and the Textus Receptus) read poiountes tas entolas autou (doing his commandments); the UBS/NA28 critical text prefers plynnontes tas stolas autōn (washing their robes), supported by Sinaiticus and Alexandrinus. The robes-washing image echoes 7:14 (they washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb) — the passive work of atonement rather than active commandment-keeping. Both readings convey holiness, but the robes-washing variant locates holiness in Christ's atoning work.

hina estai hē exousia autōn epi to xylon tēs zōēs kai tois pylōsin eiselthōsin eis tēn polin: exousia (authority, right, legal standing) to the tree of life and entry through the gates. The legal term exousia (not merely dynamis = power) signals that the right to enter is forensic: it is granted, not seized. The tree of life access denied in Gen 3:24 is restored by the Lamb's atoning work that makes the robe-washers clean.
15: exō hoi kynes kai hoi pharmakoi kai hoi pornoi kai hoi phoneis kai hoi eidōlolatrai kai pas philōn kai poiōn pseudos: outside the city are five categories plus "all who love and practice falsehood." Kynes (dogs): in Jewish purity discourse, dogs were ritually unclean animals associated with those who returned to their vomit (2 Pet 2:22; Prov 26:11) and used metaphorically for those who had abandoned the faith or remained outside the covenant community. Phil 3:2 uses kynas for the circumcision party; here it is the final-state category for those outside the new Jerusalem. The list mirrors 21:8. Pas philōn kai poiōn pseudos (everyone who loves and makes falsehood) — the participial construction indicates characteristic love-of-lying, not occasional lapse; the disposition, not the act alone.
16:
ego Iēsous epempsa ton angelon mou martyrēsai hymin tauta epi tais ekklēsiais: Jesus identifies himself as the sender of the angel — the one who commissioned the entire revelation. Hymin (to you, plural) addresses the seven churches collectively. Martyrēsai (to testify, bear witness) — the entire Apocalypse is framed as divine testimony, not human speculation.

ego eimi hē rhiza kai to genos David ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos: two messianic identifications. Hē rhiza kai to genos David (the root and the offspring of David) — root = David's ancestor (Isa 11:10: the root of Jesse as the one the nations seek), offspring = David's descendant (Isa 11:1: a shoot from the stump of Jesse). Jesus is both ancestor and descendant: as divine Son he is the source of the Davidic line; as incarnate Son he is David's descendant. The title in Rev 5:5 (rhiza David) here expanded. Ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos (the bright morning star): the morning star (Venus) appears just before dawn — a herald of the coming day. Num 24:17 (a star shall come out of Jacob) is the messianic star prophecy; here Christ applies it to himself as the harbinger of the eschatological dawn.
17:
kai to Pneuma kai hē Nymphē legousin Erchou: "The Spirit and the Bride say, Come!" Two voices united in the eschatological invitation: the Spirit (divine, the one who has been speaking to the churches throughout chs. 2–3: "what the Spirit says to the churches") and the Bride (the church, the new Jerusalem of ch. 21). Their unified cry Erchou (Come!) is addressed to Christ, the returning Lord.

kai ho akouōn eipatō Erchou, kai ho dipsōn erchasthō, ho thelōn labetō hydōr zōēs dōrean: the congregation joins (let the one who hears say Come), then the invitation opens to all who are thirsty (let the thirsty come, let the willing take the water of life as a gift). The final invitation of the Bible: dōrean (freely, as gift — same word as 21:6). This is the closing of John 7:37-38 (Jesus on the last day of the feast: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink") and Isa 55:1 (come to the waters, all you who are thirsty) extended to the entire canon's close.
18: martyrō egō panti tō akouonti tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: martyrō (I testify, I solemnly warn — the word of authoritative witness). The warning against adding or subtracting echoes the Deuteronomy formula (Deut 4:2: "You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it"; Deut 12:32) and the same formula applied to the book of Proverbs (Prov 30:6). Revelation here asserts canonical finality: this prophetic scroll is closed, complete, not to be supplemented or reduced. The gravity of the warning (prosthēsei ho theos ep auton tas plēgas — God will add to him the plagues written in this book) claims Torah-level authority for the Apocalypse.
19: kai ean tis aphelē apo tōn logōn tou bibliou tēs prophēteias tautēs, aphelei ho theos to meros autou apo tou xylou tēs zōēs kai ek tēs poleōs tēs hagias: the negative counterpart — taking away from the book results in losing one's share in the tree of life and the holy city. Meros (share, portion) of the tree of life is the inheritance language: the subtracter loses their inheritance in the new Jerusalem. The symmetry of addition and subtraction penalties mirrors the canonical seriousness: the integrity of the prophetic text is coextensive with the integrity of one's standing in the new creation. Textual variant: some MSS read xylou zōēs (tree of life) vs. bibliou zōēs (book of life); the former fits the new Jerusalem imagery of 22:2 and 22:14 better.
21: hē charis tou Kyriou Iēsou meta pantōn: "The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all." The Pauline epistolary benediction (cf. 1 Cor 16:23; Gal 6:18; Phil 4:23; 1 Thess 5:28; 2 Thess 3:18; Philem 25) applied to the Apocalypse — fitting, since Revelation is framed as a letter to the seven churches (1:4). Charis (grace) is the final word of the NT canon in the manuscript tradition. The entire Bible ends with grace: not command, not judgment, not prediction, but the free gift of God in Christ extending to "all." Pantōn (all) without qualifier — the grace of the Lord Jesus is offered without restriction.
